EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with a wave of low pressure moving along it into northern Ohio produced strong storms with heavy rain on July 27, 2014. High moisture content over the region, as indicated by dew points in the upper 60s, combined with some upslope and lake enhancement to cause flash floods. Some of the stronger storms produced strong damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Starting around 520 PM on the evening of the 27th storms associated with a cold front moving through the region produced torrential rain over the Middlefield area. Between 545 and 715 PM 4.13 of rain fell as reported by the CoCoRaHS observer in Middlefield. It was this heavy rain that caused the Tare Creek to overcome it's banks and flood a retirement community and an apartment complex. This was the first ever reported incident of the creek flooding the retirement home which was built in 1965. The flood waters filled the basement with seven feet of water and resulted in evacuations. Further downstream sat an apartment complex where the Fire Department performed water rescues of 8 residents. The flood waters were estimated at eight inches deep in the apartments. An additional 0.9 of rain fell between 8 PM and 8 AM but no additional flash flooding resulted. Numerous homes in the community had flooded basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a downbust over the northwestern corner of Mahoning County.  Winds estimated at more than 60 mph downed many trees at a campground between Lake Milton and Berlin Lake.  Several people were briefly trapped in the campground because trees blocked several roads.  No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a downbust over the northwestern corner of Mahoning County.  Winds estimated at more than 60 mph downed many trees at a campground between Lake Milton and Berlin Lake.  Several people were briefly trapped in the campground because trees blocked several roads.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of northern Stark County Ohio saw a series of thunderstorms with intense rain over three days. The first storm occurred primarily over the Canton/Northern Canton area with rainfall amounts around 3 inches.  Between 4 AM and 6 AM on the morning of the 18th another thunderstorm rolled through north/northeast Stark County. This line of storms brought 1 to 1.5 inches of rainfall notably over the Louisville area. Later in the evening of the 18th a storm brought 1 to 1.5 inches to north/northwest Stark County. Each event unfolded with flash floods, the later ones enhanced by already saturated ground cover and backed up rivers and tributaries of the Nimishillen River. Overall an estimated 1,000 sustained some form of water damage, mostly in basements. Dozens of businesses were inundated and dozens of people were evacuated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 and 6 AM on the morning of June 18th heavy rain moved over the Summit and Stark county-line. The measured rainfall amounts for 24 hours ending at 7 am that morning were: 2.09 at Akron/Canton airport, 2.35 at Berlin Lake, 1.84 in Barberton, 3.90 in Uniontown and Mayfair Road. The great majority of the rain fell that morning. This rainfall fell on already saturated ground cover from flooding less than 36 hours earlier. The East Branch of the Nimishillen Creek could not handle the excessive runoff and it flooded a large portion of Louisville. Several businesses and homes were inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across Ohio on June 11th ahead of a low pressure system located near Chicago. Instability was fairly low but enhanced winds moving through an upper level trough aloft resulted in a severe thunderstorm producing damaging winds that tracked from near Youngstown in Mahoning County to Hubbard in Trumbull County. Numerous trees were uprooted with this storm. Damage was also reported in Mill Creek Metropark where several roads were closed due to downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were brought down by strong thunderstorm winds in Hubbard.  One tree fell onto a pick-up truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved northeast across the Central Great Lakes on September 10th. A warm front stretching southeast from this low lifted across northern Ohio causing showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. At least four weak tornadoes touched down in Summit and Portage Counties. Several funnel clouds were also reported in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down on the Stow-Munroe Falls High School property.  No damage was observed at the high school.  The tornado continued northeast for about one half mile and finally lifted near the intersection of Berwin Drive and Bunker Lake in the Quail Highlands neighborhood.  The roof and chimney of one house was heavily damaged by the tornado.  Four other houses nearby lost sections of roofing or siding.  Many trees were also downed along the damage path which was no more than 50 yards in width.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved northeast across the Central Great Lakes on September 10th. A warm front stretching southeast from this low lifted across northern Ohio causing showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. At least four weak tornadoes touched down in Summit and Portage Counties. Several funnel clouds were also reported in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same thunderstorm that produced a weak tornado in Stow also produced a tornado a few miles away near the small village of Sugar Bush Knolls in western Portage County.  This EF0 tornado initially touched down near the intersection of Spell Road and Judson Road.  The tornado continued northeast for nearly a mile and crossed the Raccoon Hill Golf Club.  The tornado eventually lifted after crossing Hudson Road.   Many trees were downed along the damage patch which was up to 50 yards in width.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved northeast across the Central Great Lakes on September 10th. A warm front stretching southeast from this low lifted across northern Ohio causing showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. At least four weak tornadoes touched down in Summit and Portage Counties. Several funnel clouds were also reported in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very weak EF0 tornado touched down near Streetsboro.  The touch down occurred just south of Lake Rockwell Road to the west of State Route 14.  The tornado was on the ground for about a tenth of a mile and lifted before reaching State Route 14.  The tornado downed one tree and tore the branches off a couple other trees.  The damage path was no more than 20 yards in width.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm moist air ahead of a slow moving cold front supported storms with torrential rain June 23rd through midday on the 25th. Numerous thunderstorms produced rainfall amounts around 1 to 2 inches during that time. For areas that saw training thunderstorms rainfall amounts exceeded 4 inches.  Heavy rain and floods the week before had kept grounds saturated in many areas, so this additional rainfall was sufficient to cause flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall around 2 to 4 inches fell over Lorain County on the evening of June 25th. The first round of showers began from 3 to 5 p.m. and was the  heaviest from 7 to 9 p.m. The highest amount measured was 3.72 inches in Elyria. This resulted in significant flooding to the area with numerous homes inundated. In Elyria about 8:55 p.m., a manhole cover popped off on Gulf Road, leaving a hole ���big enough for a submarine,��� a dispatcher���s transmission said. At 9:20 p.m., state Route 57 north between state Route 254 and Schadden Road in Elyria was blocked off, said police Lt. Lee Frank. Martin's Run Creek in Lorain caused flooding of several streets between West 35th Street and Oberlin Avenue. The Oberlin Police Department confirmed there were areas of flooding all throughout the city of Oberlin. Oberlin streets were blocked in many areas because of high water, including Prospect Street, North Main Street and South Professor Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines down in Mill Creek MetroPark and across Youngstown. Some trees were uprooted. The following roads were closed due to downed trees; West Golf Drive, East Park Drive, Chestnut Hill, West Drive, East Glacier, Valley Drive, and the Flats Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the Great Lakes generated some showers and thunderstorms on Saturday August 2nd. The slow moving nature of these thunderstorms combined with rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr at times caused flash flooding over eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Cortland measured rainfall rates around 4 inches per hour on the afternoon of the 2nd. This intense rainfall fell for 45 minutes. Radar estimated rainfall amounts were between 2 and 3 inches for the storm total. The impacts included a washout on State Route 46. The Walnut Creek overflowed it's banks and covered portions of Old Oak Drive with over a foot of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the Great Lakes generated some showers and thunderstorms on Saturday August 2nd. The slow moving nature of these thunderstorms combined with rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr at times caused flash flooding over eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over southern portions of Trumbull County on the afternoon of the 2nd. Rainfall rates were measured at around 4/hr earlier in the storm, and conditions appeared to be unchanged when the storm moved over the town of Girard. The torrential rain caused flooding on U.S. Route 422 at Smithsonian Street, Kline and Broadway, Patricia Drive and Park Avenue. There was also considerable basement and yard flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic blast on January 6th and 7th dropped temperatures around -10F to -15F. This severe cold snap iced over most of Lake Erie and almost every river and creek in the region. The ice in the western rivers like the Maumee and Portage grew to 8-10��� thick, while eastern rivers saw ice thicknesses around 6���. The cold snap was followed by rain and extremely mild temperatures in the 40s and 50s, resulting in many breakup jams. The rainfall that fell during the thaw averaged 0.75��� and the snowmelt another 0.1-0.5���. This runoff was enough to raise water levels, but only a few reached or exceeded flood stage. Had the rainfall been even a half inch more, than most rivers would have flooded which could have made the ice jams all the more severe. However, for the most part the ice jams were small and low impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam stretching from the railroad bridge south of the SR 2 bridge to the Chargrin River Park closed several roads and the park itself as water and ice came out of the riverbed. Lost Nation Road was shut down between Pelton Road and Apollo Parkway in Willoughby as well as Erie Street between Gilson Park and St. Clair Street. Roads by Daniels Park were also closed. Though some residents evacuated, no homes were flooded. Two people had to be rescued after driving into a flooded road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators reported the intersection of County Road 3 and E were closed due to down power lines and poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under high pressure, a clear sky, and a light wind, temperatures dropped below zero across a large portion of southeast Ohio for dawn on the 11th.||A spotter reported 13 below zero in Mt Perry of northern Perry County. Unofficial readings around 10 below zero were registered at the deparment of highways garage near New Lexington of Perry County. A temperature of minus 10 was also measured in Watertown of western Washington County.  The Ohio University registered a minimum temperature of 8 below zero.  In Vinton County, Zaleski had a temperature of 7 below zero.||Further south, readings of zero to minus 5 were more common across Gallia and Jackson Counties.

Low pressure tracked across the northern Great Lakes on January 26 and 27th, pulling an arctic cold front east across the area during the early morning hours of January 27th. Temperatures ahead of the front were in the upper 30s and lower 40s, falling through the day on the 27th with dangerously cold wind chill values developing during the early morning hours of the 28th.  West to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with temperatures as low as -15 degrees to produce the wind chill conditions.  Findlay recorded the coldest wind chill value reported in northern Ohio of -33 degrees at 553 am on the 28th.  Many locations recorded the very cold wind chills between 1 am and 10 am before temperatures started to recover.  Also noteworthy were record low temperatures set at Toledo and Mansfield of -10 and -15 degrees respectively, breaking the previous records from 1977.  Temperatures only recovered into the single digits during the day on the 28th and then fell to sub zero values again overnight.  Nearly all schools were closed on the 28th with most school also closed on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dangerously cold air mass settled over northern Ohio from January 27-28th with wind chill values of -25 to -30 degrees during the early morning hours of January 28th. The wind chill dropped to -28 degrees at Port Clinton and -29 degrees at Toledo between 6 and 8 am on January 28th with west to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph. A record low temperature of -10 degrees was set that morning at Toledo and tied the previous record for that day from 1977. Conditions started to improve by late morning as temperatures gradually recovered into the single digits but dropped below zero again overnight. All area school were closed on January 28th with most school also closed on January 29th.

Late on the 16th, a low pressure storm system passed east across the Tennessee Valley, while strong high pressure was located over the Great Lakes and southern Ontario. Southeast Ohio was on the northern fringe of the precipitation shield.  Snow was heaviest in extreme southern Ohio during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 16th.  The snow diminished during the predawn hours on the 17th.  Snow accumulations of 4 to 5 inches were widespread across Lawrence and Gallia Counties, mostly in a 12 hour period.  For example, the cooperative observer at Gallipolis measured 5 inches.   Snow amounts of an inch or less were seen further north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure moved east across the Great Lakes on January 25th.  A cold front trailing this low swept east across northern Ohio during the morning hours.  Snow developed ahead of this front during the early morning and then intensified around daybreak as the front moved through.  Southwest winds gusted to as much as 35 mph ahead of the front.  Northwest winds developed behind the frontal passage and by midday the snow had transitioned to lake effect snow showers.  The snow showers intensified just before the evening commute causing near blizzard conditions at times.  Visibilities were less than a quarter mile for most of the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour crippled the Cleveland Metropolitan area and made for a rough evening commute.  The snow lessened around 7 pm and tapered to flurries shortly after that.  Snowfall totals across Cuyahoga, Geauga and northern Summit and Medina Counties ranged from 6 to locally 9 inches.  Much of that fell during the afternoon.  In Cuyahoga County totals included 10.0 inches at Broadview Heights; 9.0 inches at Solon; 7.5 inches in Garfield Heights; 7.0 inches in Lakewood and 6.8 inches in Parma.   In Geauga County, a peak total of 12.5 inches was reported in Thompson with 11.1 inches at Chagrin Falls and 8.0 inches at Burton.  In Medina County a peak total of 7.9 inches was measured near Medina with 7.5 inches in Wadsworth and 6.0 inches in Brunswick.  The northern and central portions of Summit County also saw a lot snow with a peak total of 10.7 inches at Sagamore Hills.  Other totals from Summit County included: 8.5 inches at Stow; 7.5 inches in Akron; 7.1 inches at Cuyahoga Falls and 6.9 inches at Tallmadge.  In addition, the gusty winds caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Dozens of accidents were reported across the area and commute times were two to three times normal.

Snow developed ahead of this front during the early morning and then intensified around daybreak as the front moved through. Southwest winds gusted to as much as 35 mph ahead of the front. Northwest winds developed behind the frontal passage and by midday the snow had transitioned to lake effect snow showers. The snow showers intensified just before the evening commute causing near blizzard conditions at times. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour crippled the Cleveland Metropolitan area and made for a rough evening commute. The snow lessened around 7 pm and tapered to flurries shortly after that. In Cuyahoga County totals included 10.0 inches at Broadview Heights; 9.0 inches at Solon; 7.5 inches in Garfield Heights; 7.0 inches in Lakewood and 6.8 inches in Parma. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 5th. This low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 6th and then continued rapidly northeast.  Snow associated with this low developed over northwestern Ohio around daybreak on the 5th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were then reported throughout the day.  Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour at times.  The snow briefly ended during the early morning hours of the 6th but resumed again after daybreak.  The snow ended for good around midday on the 6th.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph on the 5th with westerly winds in excess of 40 mph on the 6th.  Near blizzard conditions were reported for many hours and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Dozens of accidents were reported.   Snowfall totals of 6 inches or more were reported as far south as Hancock County and as far east as eastern Ottawa and Sandusky Counties.  The heaviest snow fell across western Lucas County.  At Toledo Express Airport a total of 13.0 inches fell on the 5th and 6th with 9.4 inches on 5th.  Other storm totals included 9.3 inches at Sylvania in Lucas County and 9.0 inches at Findlay in Hancock County.  Temperatures plummeted on the 6th with temperatures falling below zero by mid morning.  The combination of frigid temperatures and strong winds made clean up of the snow nearly impossible.  Schools in Northwest Ohio were closed on both the 6th and 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed over northwestern Ohio around daybreak on the 5th. Periods of moderate to heavy snow were then reported throughout the day. Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour at times. The snow briefly ended during the early morning hours of the 6th but resumed again after daybreak. The snow ended for good around midday on the 6th. Northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph on the 5th with westerly winds in excess of 40 mph on the 6th. Near blizzard conditions were reported for many hours and travel was nearly impossible at times.  The heaviest snow fell across western Lucas County. At Toledo Express Airport a total of 13.0 inches fell on the 5th and 6th with 9.4 inches on 5th. 9.3 inches of snow fell at Sylvania. Temperatures plummeted on the 6th with temperatures falling below zero by mid morning. The combination of frigid temperatures and strong winds made clean up of the snow nearly impossible. Schools in Northwest Ohio were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley on the 6th, bringing record low temperatures and extreme wind chills the morning of the 7th. It was the coldest January 7th on record in Pittsburgh with a low temperature of 9 below zero, and a high temperature of 4 above zero. Across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, low temperatures ranged from 5 to 15 degrees below zero the morning of the 7th, with the lowest wind chill readings from 25 to 55 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of 14 degrees below zero with a wind chill of 36 degrees below zero was recorded near Carrollton the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 5th. This low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 6th and then continued rapidly northeast.  Snow associated with this low developed over northwestern Ohio around daybreak on the 5th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were then reported throughout the day.  Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour at times.  The snow briefly ended during the early morning hours of the 6th but resumed again after daybreak.  The snow ended for good around midday on the 6th.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph on the 5th with westerly winds in excess of 40 mph on the 6th.  Near blizzard conditions were reported for many hours and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Dozens of accidents were reported.   Snowfall totals of 6 inches or more were reported as far south as Hancock County and as far east as eastern Ottawa and Sandusky Counties.  The heaviest snow fell across western Lucas County.  At Toledo Express Airport a total of 13.0 inches fell on the 5th and 6th with 9.4 inches on 5th.  Other storm totals included 9.3 inches at Sylvania in Lucas County and 9.0 inches at Findlay in Hancock County.  Temperatures plummeted on the 6th with temperatures falling below zero by mid morning.  The combination of frigid temperatures and strong winds made clean up of the snow nearly impossible.  Schools in Northwest Ohio were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late on the 16th, a low pressure storm system passed east across the Tennessee Valley, while strong high pressure was located over the Great Lakes and southern Ontario. Southeast Ohio was on the northern fringe of the precipitation shield.  Snow was heaviest in extreme southern Ohio during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 16th.  The snow diminished during the predawn hours on the 17th.  Snow accumulations of 4 to 5 inches were widespread across Lawrence and Gallia Counties, mostly in a 12 hour period.  For example, the cooperative observer at Gallipolis measured 5 inches.   Snow amounts of an inch or less were seen further north.

An area of strong low pressure moved east across the Great Lakes on January 25th.  A cold front trailing this low swept east across northern Ohio during the morning hours.  Snow developed ahead of this front during the early morning and then intensified around daybreak as the front moved through.  Southwest winds gusted to as much as 35 mph ahead of the front.  Northwest winds developed behind the frontal passage and by midday the snow had transitioned to lake effect snow showers.  The snow showers intensified just before the evening commute causing near blizzard conditions at times.  Visibilities were less than a quarter mile for most of the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour crippled the Cleveland Metropolitan area and made for a rough evening commute.  The snow lessened around 7 pm and tapered to flurries shortly after that.  Snowfall totals across Cuyahoga, Geauga and northern Summit and Medina Counties ranged from 6 to locally 9 inches.  Much of that fell during the afternoon.  In Cuyahoga County totals included 10.0 inches at Broadview Heights; 9.0 inches at Solon; 7.5 inches in Garfield Heights; 7.0 inches in Lakewood and 6.8 inches in Parma.   In Geauga County, a peak total of 12.5 inches was reported in Thompson with 11.1 inches at Chagrin Falls and 8.0 inches at Burton.  In Medina County a peak total of 7.9 inches was measured near Medina with 7.5 inches in Wadsworth and 6.0 inches in Brunswick.  The northern and central portions of Summit County also saw a lot snow with a peak total of 10.7 inches at Sagamore Hills.  Other totals from Summit County included: 8.5 inches at Stow; 7.5 inches in Akron; 7.1 inches at Cuyahoga Falls and 6.9 inches at Tallmadge.  In addition, the gusty winds caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Dozens of accidents were reported across the area and commute times were two to three times normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 5th.  This low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 6th and then continued rapidly northeast.  At the same time, arctic high pressure settled over the Great Plains resulting in a very strong pressure gradient over the Upper Ohio Valley.  Strong west to northwest winds ushered in an unusually cold and dangerous airmass and resulted in the coldest temperatures in northern Ohio since January 1994.  Record low temperatures and unprecedented wind chills were reported throughout the region on both January 6th and 7th.  Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph on the 6th.  By daybreak on the 7th, sustained winds had dropped to 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 30 mph but this provided only slight relief from the brutal wind chills.  Temperatures during the evening of the 5th were in the 30s across all of northern Ohio.  By daybreak on the 6th, readings were below zero in northwest Ohio and in the single digits and teens elsewhere.  My midday, subzero readings were reported across all of northern Ohio with readings of 10 below or colder at many locations by early evening.  Wind chills dropped to below negative 25 by mid morning in northwestern Ohio and by early afternoon in northeastern Ohio.   Wind chills eventually dipped below minus 40 during the evening hours near Toledo and Findlay.  Wind chills elsewhere were less than minus 35 degrees for a 10 to 12 hour period.  Wind chills finally warmed above minus 25 during the early afternoon hours of the 7th.  The coldest wind chill observed was -43 degrees at both Toledo and Findlay around 7 pm on the 6th.  Mansfield, Lorain and Cleveland saw wind chills dip to minus 39 for several hours.  All of the other observing sites saw wind chills fall to as low as minus 37.  There were at least two cold related deaths reported across the area.  All schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.  All five of the major climate sites in northern Ohio established new record lows on January 6th.  The old records at both Toledo and Cleveland were from 1884.  The record lows on the 6th were as follows:  -15 at Toledo; -13 at Mansfield; -11 at Cleveland; -11 at Youngstown and -10 at Akron-Canton.  Record lows for the 7th included: -14 at Toledo; -12 at Mansfield; -12 at Youngstown; -11 at Cleveland and -11 at Akron-Canton.  Low temperatures at the other automated sensors in northern Ohio were also -10 degrees or colder on both the 6th and 7th.  High temperatures on the 7th were only in the lower single digits above zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brutal cold weather settled over the area on January 6th and 7th. The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were reported with lows of minus 10 or colder on both the 6th and 7th. Wind chills dipped below minus 25 degrees by midday on the 6th and then continued for about 24 hours. Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph. All of the schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the northern Great Lakes on January 26 and 27th, pulling an arctic cold front east across the area during the early morning hours of January 27th. Temperatures ahead of the front were in the upper 30s and lower 40s, falling through the day on the 27th with dangerously cold wind chill values developing during the early morning hours of the 28th.  West to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with temperatures as low as -15 degrees to produce the wind chill conditions.  Findlay recorded the coldest wind chill value reported in northern Ohio of -33 degrees at 553 am on the 28th.  Many locations recorded the very cold wind chills between 1 am and 10 am before temperatures started to recover.  Also noteworthy were record low temperatures set at Toledo and Mansfield of -10 and -15 degrees respectively, breaking the previous records from 1977.  Temperatures only recovered into the single digits during the day on the 28th and then fell to sub zero values again overnight.  Nearly all schools were closed on the 28th with most school also closed on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dangerously cold air mass settled over northern Ohio from January 27-28th with wind chill values of -25 to -30 degrees during the early morning hours of January 28th. The wind chill dropped to -33 at Findlay and -32 at Marion between 6 and 8 am  on January 28th with west to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph. A record low temperature of -10 degrees was set that morning at Toledo and tied the previous record for that day from 1977. A record low of -15 also occurred at Mansfield and broke the record of -14 from 1977.  Conditions started to improve by late morning as temperatures gradually recovered into the single digits but dropped below zero again overnight. All area school were closed on January 28th and most were closed on January 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure moved east across the Great Lakes on January 25th.  A cold front trailing this low swept east across northern Ohio during the morning hours.  Snow developed ahead of this front during the early morning and then intensified around daybreak as the front moved through.  Southwest winds gusted to as much as 35 mph ahead of the front.  Northwest winds developed behind the frontal passage and by midday the snow had transitioned to lake effect snow showers.  The snow showers intensified just before the evening commute causing near blizzard conditions at times.  Visibilities were less than a quarter mile for most of the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour crippled the Cleveland Metropolitan area and made for a rough evening commute.  The snow lessened around 7 pm and tapered to flurries shortly after that.  Snowfall totals across Cuyahoga, Geauga and northern Summit and Medina Counties ranged from 6 to locally 9 inches.  Much of that fell during the afternoon.  In Cuyahoga County totals included 10.0 inches at Broadview Heights; 9.0 inches at Solon; 7.5 inches in Garfield Heights; 7.0 inches in Lakewood and 6.8 inches in Parma.   In Geauga County, a peak total of 12.5 inches was reported in Thompson with 11.1 inches at Chagrin Falls and 8.0 inches at Burton.  In Medina County a peak total of 7.9 inches was measured near Medina with 7.5 inches in Wadsworth and 6.0 inches in Brunswick.  The northern and central portions of Summit County also saw a lot snow with a peak total of 10.7 inches at Sagamore Hills.  Other totals from Summit County included: 8.5 inches at Stow; 7.5 inches in Akron; 7.1 inches at Cuyahoga Falls and 6.9 inches at Tallmadge.  In addition, the gusty winds caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Dozens of accidents were reported across the area and commute times were two to three times normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 5th.  This low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 6th and then continued rapidly northeast.  At the same time, arctic high pressure settled over the Great Plains resulting in a very strong pressure gradient over the Upper Ohio Valley.  Strong west to northwest winds ushered in an unusually cold and dangerous airmass and resulted in the coldest temperatures in northern Ohio since January 1994.  Record low temperatures and unprecedented wind chills were reported throughout the region on both January 6th and 7th.  Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph on the 6th.  By daybreak on the 7th, sustained winds had dropped to 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 30 mph but this provided only slight relief from the brutal wind chills.  Temperatures during the evening of the 5th were in the 30s across all of northern Ohio.  By daybreak on the 6th, readings were below zero in northwest Ohio and in the single digits and teens elsewhere.  My midday, subzero readings were reported across all of northern Ohio with readings of 10 below or colder at many locations by early evening.  Wind chills dropped to below negative 25 by mid morning in northwestern Ohio and by early afternoon in northeastern Ohio.   Wind chills eventually dipped below minus 40 during the evening hours near Toledo and Findlay.  Wind chills elsewhere were less than minus 35 degrees for a 10 to 12 hour period.  Wind chills finally warmed above minus 25 during the early afternoon hours of the 7th.  The coldest wind chill observed was -43 degrees at both Toledo and Findlay around 7 pm on the 6th.  Mansfield, Lorain and Cleveland saw wind chills dip to minus 39 for several hours.  All of the other observing sites saw wind chills fall to as low as minus 37.  There were at least two cold related deaths reported across the area.  All schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.  All five of the major climate sites in northern Ohio established new record lows on January 6th.  The old records at both Toledo and Cleveland were from 1884.  The record lows on the 6th were as follows:  -15 at Toledo; -13 at Mansfield; -11 at Cleveland; -11 at Youngstown and -10 at Akron-Canton.  Record lows for the 7th included: -14 at Toledo; -12 at Mansfield; -12 at Youngstown; -11 at Cleveland and -11 at Akron-Canton.  Low temperatures at the other automated sensors in northern Ohio were also -10 degrees or colder on both the 6th and 7th.  High temperatures on the 7th were only in the lower single digits above zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brutal cold weather settled over the area on January 6th and 7th. The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were reported with lows of minus 10 or colder on both the 6th and 7th. Low temperatures at the Findlay Airport on the 6th and 7th were -14 and -14.  The coldest wind chill reported at the Findlay Airport was -43 around 7 pm on the 6th.  Wind chills dipped below minus 25 degrees by midday on the 6th and then continued for about 24 hours. Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph. All of the schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 5th. This low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 6th and then continued rapidly northeast.  Snow associated with this low developed over northwestern Ohio around daybreak on the 5th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were then reported throughout the day.  Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour at times.  The snow briefly ended during the early morning hours of the 6th but resumed again after daybreak.  The snow ended for good around midday on the 6th.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph on the 5th with westerly winds in excess of 40 mph on the 6th.  Near blizzard conditions were reported for many hours and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Dozens of accidents were reported.   Snowfall totals of 6 inches or more were reported as far south as Hancock County and as far east as eastern Ottawa and Sandusky Counties.  The heaviest snow fell across western Lucas County.  At Toledo Express Airport a total of 13.0 inches fell on the 5th and 6th with 9.4 inches on 5th.  Other storm totals included 9.3 inches at Sylvania in Lucas County and 9.0 inches at Findlay in Hancock County.  Temperatures plummeted on the 6th with temperatures falling below zero by mid morning.  The combination of frigid temperatures and strong winds made clean up of the snow nearly impossible.  Schools in Northwest Ohio were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

Near blizzard conditions were reported for many hours and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Snowfall totals across Hancock County ranged from 6 to 9 inches with the higher amounts across the western end of the county.  A snow spotter in Findlay measured 9.0 inches of snow.  Temperatures plummeted on the 6th with temperatures falling below zero by mid morning. The combination of frigid temperatures and strong winds made clean up of the snow nearly impossible. Schools in Northwest Ohio were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

Nearly all schools were closed on the 28th with most school also closed on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dangerously cold air mass settled over northern Ohio from January 27-28th with wind chill values of -25 to -35 degrees during the early morning hours of January 28th. The wind chill dropped to -28 at Wooster and -30 at Akron-Canton between 4 and 9 am with west to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph. A record low temperature of -15 degrees occurred at Mansfield and broke the record of -14 from 1977. Conditions started to improve by late morning as temperatures gradually recovered into the single digits but dropped below zero again overnight. All area school were closed on January 28th and most were closed on January 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 5th. This low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 6th and then continued rapidly northeast.  Snow associated with this low developed over northwestern Ohio around daybreak on the 5th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were then reported throughout the day.  Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour at times.  The snow briefly ended during the early morning hours of the 6th but resumed again after daybreak.  The snow ended for good around midday on the 6th.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph on the 5th with westerly winds in excess of 40 mph on the 6th.  Near blizzard conditions were reported for many hours and travel was nearly impossible at times.  Dozens of accidents were reported.   Snowfall totals of 6 inches or more were reported as far south as Hancock County and as far east as eastern Ottawa and Sandusky Counties.  The heaviest snow fell across western Lucas County.  At Toledo Express Airport a total of 13.0 inches fell on the 5th and 6th with 9.4 inches on 5th.  Other storm totals included 9.3 inches at Sylvania in Lucas County and 9.0 inches at Findlay in Hancock County.  Temperatures plummeted on the 6th with temperatures falling below zero by mid morning.  The combination of frigid temperatures and strong winds made clean up of the snow nearly impossible.  Schools in Northwest Ohio were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the northern Great Lakes on January 26 and 27th, pulling an arctic cold front east across the area during the early morning hours of January 27th. Temperatures ahead of the front were in the upper 30s and lower 40s, falling through the day on the 27th with dangerously cold wind chill values developing during the early morning hours of the 28th.  West to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with temperatures as low as -15 degrees to produce the wind chill conditions.  Findlay recorded the coldest wind chill value reported in northern Ohio of -33 degrees at 553 am on the 28th.  Many locations recorded the very cold wind chills between 1 am and 10 am before temperatures started to recover.  Also noteworthy were record low temperatures set at Toledo and Mansfield of -10 and -15 degrees respectively, breaking the previous records from 1977. A record low temperature of -15 degrees occurred at Mansfield and broke the record of -14 from 1977. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 5th.  This low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 6th and then continued rapidly northeast.  At the same time, arctic high pressure settled over the Great Plains resulting in a very strong pressure gradient over the Upper Ohio Valley.  Strong west to northwest winds ushered in an unusually cold and dangerous airmass and resulted in the coldest temperatures in northern Ohio since January 1994.  Record low temperatures and unprecedented wind chills were reported throughout the region on both January 6th and 7th.  Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph on the 6th.  By daybreak on the 7th, sustained winds had dropped to 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 30 mph but this provided only slight relief from the brutal wind chills.  Temperatures during the evening of the 5th were in the 30s across all of northern Ohio.  By daybreak on the 6th, readings were below zero in northwest Ohio and in the single digits and teens elsewhere.  My midday, subzero readings were reported across all of northern Ohio with readings of 10 below or colder at many locations by early evening.  Wind chills dropped to below negative 25 by mid morning in northwestern Ohio and by early afternoon in northeastern Ohio.   Wind chills eventually dipped below minus 40 during the evening hours near Toledo and Findlay.  Wind chills elsewhere were less than minus 35 degrees for a 10 to 12 hour period.  Wind chills finally warmed above minus 25 during the early afternoon hours of the 7th.  The coldest wind chill observed was -43 degrees at both Toledo and Findlay around 7 pm on the 6th.  Mansfield, Lorain and Cleveland saw wind chills dip to minus 39 for several hours.  All of the other observing sites saw wind chills fall to as low as minus 37.  There were at least two cold related deaths reported across the area.  All schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.  All five of the major climate sites in northern Ohio established new record lows on January 6th.  The old records at both Toledo and Cleveland were from 1884.  The record lows on the 6th were as follows:  -15 at Toledo; -13 at Mansfield; -11 at Cleveland; -11 at Youngstown and -10 at Akron-Canton.  Record lows for the 7th included: -14 at Toledo; -12 at Mansfield; -12 at Youngstown; -11 at Cleveland and -11 at Akron-Canton.  Low temperatures at the other automated sensors in northern Ohio were also -10 degrees or colder on both the 6th and 7th.  High temperatures on the 7th were only in the lower single digits above zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brutal cold weather settled over the area on January 6th and 7th. The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were reported with lows of minus 10 or colder on both the 6th and 7th.  Wind chills dipped below minus 25 degrees by midday on the 6th and then continued for about 24 hours. Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph. All of the schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across the Tennessee Valley on January 2nd.  A trough extending north from this low caused snow to develop just before midnight on  January 2nd.  The trough moved into western Pennsylvania during the late afternoon hours of the 2nd causing the flow to become northerly off of Lake Erie.  Snowfall intensities began to increase during the morning hours with visibilities dropping below a quarter mile for several hours during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Snowfall rates during this time approached two inches per hour.  The evening commute in the Cleveland area was very difficult and dozens of accidents were reported.  The snow finally tapered off from west to east during the late evening hours.  Northeast to north winds gusted to as much as 25 mph during this event causing a lot of blowing and drifting.  Six to eight inches of snow fell from Lorain County east across the Cleveland Metropolitan area and Lake and Geauga Counties and into Ashtabula Counties.  Much of this accumulation fell between 1 and 6 pm.  A peak total of 11.0 inches was reported at Madison in Lake County.  Other reports included 8.0 inches at Euclid in Cuyahoga County with 9.3 inches at Thompson and 8.5 inches at Montville both in Geauga County.  A peak total of 7.8 inches was reported near Lorain in Lorain County with around 6 inches of accumulation across the northern half of Ashtabula County.

A peak total of 7.8 inches was reported near Lorain in Lorain County with around 6 inches of accumulation across the northern half of Ashtabula County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across the Tennessee Valley on January 2nd. A trough extending north from this low caused snow to develop just before midnight on January 2nd. The trough moved into western Pennsylvania during the late afternoon hours of the 2nd causing the flow to become northerly off of Lake Erie. Snowfall intensities began to increase during the morning hours with visibilities dropping below a quarter mile for several hours during the afternoon and early evening hours. Snowfall rates during this time approached two inches per hour. The evening commute in the Cleveland area was very difficult and dozens of accidents were reported. The snow finally tapered off from west to east during the late evening hours. Northeast to north winds gusted to as much as 25 mph during this event causing a lot of blowing and drifting. Six to eight inches of snow fell from Lorain County east across the Cleveland Metropolitan area and Lake and Geauga Counties and into Ashtabula Counties. Much of this accumulation fell between 1 and 6 pm.  Around 6 inches of accumulation was reported across the northern half of Ashtabula County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the northern Great Lakes on January 26 and 27th, pulling an arctic cold front east across the area during the early morning hours of January 27th. Temperatures ahead of the front were in the upper 30s and lower 40s, falling through the day on the 27th with dangerously cold wind chill values developing during the early morning hours of the 28th.  West to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with temperatures as low as -15 degrees to produce the wind chill conditions.  Findlay recorded the coldest wind chill value reported in northern Ohio of -33 degrees at 553 am on the 28th.  Many locations recorded the very cold wind chills between 1 am and 10 am before temperatures started to recover.  Also noteworthy were record low temperatures set at Toledo and Mansfield of -10 and -15 degrees respectively, breaking the previous records from 1977.  Temperatures only recovered into the single digits during the day on the 28th and then fell to sub zero values again overnight.  Nearly all schools were closed on the 28th with most school also closed on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dangerously cold air mass settled over northern Ohio from January 27-28th with wind chill values of -25 to -35 degrees during the early morning hours of January 28th. The wind chill dropped to -30 at Akron-Canton between 4 and 9 am with west to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph. A record low temperature of -15 degrees occurred at Mansfield and broke the record of -14 from 1977. Conditions started to improve by late morning as temperatures gradually recovered into the single digits but dropped below zero again overnight. All area school were closed on January 28th and most were closed on January 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a major January thaw with temperatures reaching into the 50s on the 1st, a cold front gradually sank through southeast Ohio during the daylight hours on the 2nd.  Spotty light rain fell with the front. ||Meanwhile, upper level support arrived from the southwest to enhance the precipitation overnight into the 3rd.  ||The light rain changed to freezing rain and sleet, then to snow.  Accumulations of 5 to 8 inches were common in Jackson, Gallia, Meigs, Athens, Vinton, and Washington Counties. One unofficial report of a 9 inch accumulation was received fron northwest of Rio Grande in Gallia County. The state highway garage north of Pomeroy in Meigs County had 8.5 inches of accumulation.  In the extreme south, Lawrence County had 3 to 5 inches of snow accumulation.  Further north, Perry and Morgan Counties had much less snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 5th.  This low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 6th and then continued rapidly northeast.  At the same time, arctic high pressure settled over the Great Plains resulting in a very strong pressure gradient over the Upper Ohio Valley.  Strong west to northwest winds ushered in an unusually cold and dangerous airmass and resulted in the coldest temperatures in northern Ohio since January 1994.  Record low temperatures and unprecedented wind chills were reported throughout the region on both January 6th and 7th.  Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph on the 6th.  By daybreak on the 7th, sustained winds had dropped to 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 30 mph but this provided only slight relief from the brutal wind chills.  Temperatures during the evening of the 5th were in the 30s across all of northern Ohio.  By daybreak on the 6th, readings were below zero in northwest Ohio and in the single digits and teens elsewhere.  My midday, subzero readings were reported across all of northern Ohio with readings of 10 below or colder at many locations by early evening.  Wind chills dropped to below negative 25 by mid morning in northwestern Ohio and by early afternoon in northeastern Ohio.   Wind chills eventually dipped below minus 40 during the evening hours near Toledo and Findlay.  Wind chills elsewhere were less than minus 35 degrees for a 10 to 12 hour period.  Wind chills finally warmed above minus 25 during the early afternoon hours of the 7th.  The coldest wind chill observed was -43 degrees at both Toledo and Findlay around 7 pm on the 6th.  Mansfield, Lorain and Cleveland saw wind chills dip to minus 39 for several hours.  All of the other observing sites saw wind chills fall to as low as minus 37.  There were at least two cold related deaths reported across the area.  All schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.  All five of the major climate sites in northern Ohio established new record lows on January 6th.  The old records at both Toledo and Cleveland were from 1884.  The record lows on the 6th were as follows:  -15 at Toledo; -13 at Mansfield; -11 at Cleveland; -11 at Youngstown and -10 at Akron-Canton.  Record lows for the 7th included: -14 at Toledo; -12 at Mansfield; -12 at Youngstown; -11 at Cleveland and -11 at Akron-Canton.  Low temperatures at the other automated sensors in northern Ohio were also -10 degrees or colder on both the 6th and 7th.  High temperatures on the 7th were only in the lower single digits above zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brutal cold weather settled over the area on January 6th and 7th. The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were reported with lows of minus 10 or colder on both the 6th and 7th.  Wind chills dipped below minus 25 degrees by midday on the 6th and then continued for about 24 hours. Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph. All of the schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

Record low temperatures and unprecedented wind chills were reported throughout the region on both January 6th and 7th.  Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph on the 6th.  By daybreak on the 7th, sustained winds had dropped to 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 30 mph but this provided only slight relief from the brutal wind chills.  Temperatures during the evening of the 5th were in the 30s across all of northern Ohio.  By daybreak on the 6th, readings were below zero in northwest Ohio and in the single digits and teens elsewhere.  My midday, subzero readings were reported across all of northern Ohio with readings of 10 below or colder at many locations by early evening.  Wind chills dropped to below negative 25 by mid morning in northwestern Ohio and by early afternoon in northeastern Ohio.   Wind chills eventually dipped below minus 40 during the evening hours near Toledo and Findlay.  Wind chills elsewhere were less than minus 35 degrees for a 10 to 12 hour period.  Wind chills finally warmed above minus 25 during the early afternoon hours of the 7th.  The coldest wind chill observed was -43 degrees at both Toledo and Findlay around 7 pm on the 6th.  Mansfield, Lorain and Cleveland saw wind chills dip to minus 39 for several hours.  All of the other observing sites saw wind chills fall to as low as minus 37.  There were at least two cold related deaths reported across the area.  All schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.  All five of the major climate sites in northern Ohio established new record lows on January 6th.  The old records at both Toledo and Cleveland were from 1884.  The record lows on the 6th were as follows:  -15 at Toledo; -13 at Mansfield; -11 at Cleveland; -11 at Youngstown and -10 at Akron-Canton.  Record lows for the 7th included: -14 at Toledo; -12 at Mansfield; -12 at Youngstown; -11 at Cleveland and -11 at Akron-Canton.  Low temperatures at the other automated sensors in northern Ohio were also -10 degrees or colder on both the 6th and 7th.  High temperatures on the 7th were only in the lower single digits above zero.

Wind chills dipped below minus 25 degrees by midday on the 6th and then continued for about 24 hours. Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph. All of the schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across the Tennessee Valley on January 2nd.  A trough extending north from this low caused snow to develop just before midnight on  January 2nd.  The trough moved into western Pennsylvania during the late afternoon hours of the 2nd causing the flow to become northerly off of Lake Erie.  Snowfall intensities began to increase during the morning hours with visibilities dropping below a quarter mile for several hours during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Snowfall rates during this time approached two inches per hour.  The evening commute in the Cleveland area was very difficult and dozens of accidents were reported.  The snow finally tapered off from west to east during the late evening hours.  Northeast to north winds gusted to as much as 25 mph during this event causing a lot of blowing and drifting.  Six to eight inches of snow fell from Lorain County east across the Cleveland Metropolitan area and Lake and Geauga Counties and into Ashtabula Counties.  Much of this accumulation fell between 1 and 6 pm.  A peak total of 11.0 inches was reported at Madison in Lake County.  Other reports included 8.0 inches at Euclid in Cuyahoga County with 9.3 inches at Thompson and 8.5 inches at Montville both in Geauga County.  A peak total of 7.8 inches was reported near Lorain in Lorain County with around 6 inches of accumulation across the northern half of Ashtabula County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a major January thaw with temperatures reaching into the 50s on the 1st, a cold front gradually sank through southeast Ohio during the daylight hours on the 2nd.  Spotty light rain fell with the front. ||Meanwhile, upper level support arrived from the southwest to enhance the precipitation overnight into the 3rd.  ||The light rain changed to freezing rain and sleet, then to snow.  Accumulations of 5 to 8 inches were common in Jackson, Gallia, Meigs, Athens, Vinton, and Washington Counties. One unofficial report of a 9 inch accumulation was received fron northwest of Rio Grande in Gallia County. The state highway garage north of Pomeroy in Meigs County had 8.5 inches of accumulation.  In the extreme south, Lawrence County had 3 to 5 inches of snow accumulation.  Further north, Perry and Morgan Counties had much less snow.

Under high pressure, a clear sky, and a light wind, temperatures dropped below zero across a large portion of southeast Ohio for dawn on the 11th.||A spotter reported 13 below zero in Mt Perry of northern Perry County. Unofficial readings around 10 below zero were registered at the deparment of highways garage near New Lexington of Perry County. A temperature of minus 10 was also measured in Watertown of western Washington County.  The Ohio University registered a minimum temperature of 8 below zero.  In Vinton County, Zaleski had a temperature of 7 below zero.||Further south, readings of zero to minus 5 were more common across Gallia and Jackson Counties.

Low pressure tracked across the northern Great Lakes on January 26 and 27th, pulling an arctic cold front east across the area during the early morning hours of January 27th. Temperatures ahead of the front were in the upper 30s and lower 40s, falling through the day on the 27th with dangerously cold wind chill values developing during the early morning hours of the 28th.  West to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with temperatures as low as -15 degrees to produce the wind chill conditions.  Findlay recorded the coldest wind chill value reported in northern Ohio of -33 degrees at 553 am on the 28th.  Many locations recorded the very cold wind chills between 1 am and 10 am before temperatures started to recover.  Also noteworthy were record low temperatures set at Toledo and Mansfield of -10 and -15 degrees respectively, breaking the previous records from 1977.  Temperatures only recovered into the single digits during the day on the 28th and then fell to sub zero values again overnight.  Nearly all schools were closed on the 28th with most school also closed on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dangerously cold air mass settled over northern Ohio from January 27-28th with wind chill values of -25 to -30 degrees during the early morning hours of January 28th. The wind chill dropped to -26 at Cleveland Hopkins between 5 and 6 am and -28 at Akron Fulton airport around 4 am with west to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph. A record low temperature of -15 degrees occurred at Mansfield and broke the record of -14 from 1977. Conditions started to improve by late morning as temperatures gradually recovered into the single digits but dropped below zero again overnight. All area school were closed on January 28th and most were closed on January 29th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across the Tennessee Valley on January 2nd. A trough extending north from this low caused snow to develop just before midnight on January 2nd. The trough moved into western Pennsylvania during the late afternoon hours of the 2nd causing the flow to become northerly off of Lake Erie. Snowfall intensities began to increase during the morning hours with visibilities dropping below a quarter mile for several hours during the afternoon and early evening hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the northern Great Lakes on January 26 and 27th, pulling an arctic cold front east across the area during the early morning hours of January 27th. Temperatures ahead of the front were in the upper 30s and lower 40s, falling through the day on the 27th with dangerously cold wind chill values developing during the early morning hours of the 28th.  West to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with temperatures as low as -15 degrees to produce the wind chill conditions.  Findlay recorded the coldest wind chill value reported in northern Ohio of -33 degrees at 553 am on the 28th.  Many locations recorded the very cold wind chills between 1 am and 10 am before temperatures started to recover.  Also noteworthy were record low temperatures set at Toledo and Mansfield of -10 and -15 degrees respectively, breaking the previous records from 1977. Nearly all schools were closed on the 28th with most school also closed on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dangerously cold air mass settled over northern Ohio from January 27-28th with wind chill values of -25 to -30 degrees during the early morning hours of January 28th. The wind chill dropped to -26 at Ashtabula between 6 and 9 am with west to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph. A record low temperature of -15 degrees occurred at Mansfield and broke the record of -14 from 1977. Conditions started to improve by late morning as temperatures gradually recovered into the single digits but dropped below zero again overnight. All area school were closed on January 28th and most were closed on January 29th.

An area of strong low pressure moved east across the Great Lakes on January 25th.  A cold front trailing this low swept east across northern Ohio during the morning hours.  Snow developed ahead of this front during the early morning and then intensified around daybreak as the front moved through.  Southwest winds gusted to as much as 35 mph ahead of the front.  Northwest winds developed behind the frontal passage and by midday the snow had transitioned to lake effect snow showers.  The snow showers intensified just before the evening commute causing near blizzard conditions at times.  Visibilities were less than a quarter mile for most of the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour crippled the Cleveland Metropolitan area and made for a rough evening commute.  The snow lessened around 7 pm and tapered to flurries shortly after that.  Snowfall totals across Cuyahoga, Geauga and northern Summit and Medina Counties ranged from 6 to locally 9 inches.  Much of that fell during the afternoon.  In Cuyahoga County totals included 10.0 inches at Broadview Heights; 9.0 inches at Solon; 7.5 inches in Garfield Heights; 7.0 inches in Lakewood and 6.8 inches in Parma.   In Geauga County, a peak total of 12.5 inches was reported in Thompson with 11.1 inches at Chagrin Falls and 8.0 inches at Burton.  In Medina County a peak total of 7.9 inches was measured near Medina with 7.5 inches in Wadsworth and 6.0 inches in Brunswick.  The northern and central portions of Summit County also saw a lot snow with a peak total of 10.7 inches at Sagamore Hills.  Other totals from Summit County included: 8.5 inches at Stow; 7.5 inches in Akron; 7.1 inches at Cuyahoga Falls and 6.9 inches at Tallmadge.  In addition, the gusty winds caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Dozens of accidents were reported across the area and commute times were two to three times normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland the afternoon of the 25th. A heavy band of snow with the front reduced visibilities to below one quarter mile for a time as it crossed the region. Over a 12 to 18 hour period, snow showers ahead of the front, combined with several lake enhanced and up-slope snow bands behind it, produced a general 3 to 5 inches of snow across the region, with 4 to 8 inches of snow in the higher ridges of West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley on the 6th, bringing record low temperatures and extreme wind chills the morning of the 7th. It was the coldest January 7th on record in Pittsburgh with a low temperature of 9 below zero, and a high temperature of 4 above zero. Across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, low temperatures ranged from 5 to 15 degrees below zero the morning of the 7th, with the lowest wind chill readings from 25 to 55 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of 9 degrees below zero with a wind chill of 30 degrees below zero was recorded near Flushing  the morning of the 7th.

An area of very strong low pressure moved northeast across the Central Great Lakes on November 24th.  A cold front trailing this low swept east across Ohio during the morning and early afternoon hours.  A broken line of showers with embedded thunderstorms develop along the front as it moved into western Ohio.  A few of the stronger storms became severe and some damage was reported across north-central Ohio.   Southwest winds gusted to more than 60 mph behind the cold front causing damage throughout northern Ohio.  Hundreds of trees were reported down along with some utility poles.  Several semi trucks were blown over by the strong winds.  At least three injuries were reported during this evening.  Scattered power outages were reported in many areas.  Some of the higher wind gusts include 64 mph at Cleveland Hopkins International Airport in Cuyahoga County; 62 mph at Burke Lakefront Airport also in Cuyahoga County; 64 mph at the Lorain County Airport; 63 mph at the Wayne County Airport; 59 mph at both Toledo Express and Toledo Executive Airports in Lucas County and 62 mph at the Akron-Canton Airport in southern Summit County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph wind gust was measured by an automated sensor at Toledo Express Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air poured into southeast Ohio on the 27th.  A clear night with diminishing winds resulted in temperatures mostly in the minus 5 to minus 20 degree range across southeast Ohio.||At dawn on Tuesday the 28th, preliminary data indicated the coldest temperature from the official cooperative observers network was 21 degrees below zero from New Lexington. ||Some of the other minimum temperature readings around dawn on the 28th from cooperative observers included minus 9 at Jackson, minus 8 at Newport, minus 7 at Marietta, and minus 6 at Gallipolis.  ||Some unofficial readings included a minus 20 from Corning in Perry County and a minus 16 from McConnelsville in Morgan County.  A minus 14 was reported from both Zaleski in Vinton County and Watertown in Washington County.  Readings of minus 13 to minus 17 were reported around the town of Athens. ||The night of Jan 28th into Jan 29th was another bitter cold night with readings in interior southeast Ohio very similar to the previous night.  New Lexington reached minus 22 degrees, Beverly minus 16, Waterloo minus 13, Jackson minus 10.||With the Ohio River filled with broken ice, communities along the river were colder at dawn on the 29th.  Marietta observed 11 below zero. Newport reached down to 10 below zero, while Gallipolis fell to minus 8, and South Point reached minus 2.||Some unofficial readings from the 29th included minus 21 at Corning, minus 19 along Interstate 77 in northern Washington County, minus 18 at Ohio University in Athens, plus Zaleski in Vinton County and McConnelsville in Morgan County.  A minus 17 was reported in Wellston in Jackson County.       ||The 3rd clear night was not quite as bitter, but still resulted in temperatures dipping below zero in many areas for dawn on Thursday the 30th.  New Lexington had 10 below zero.  Some unofficial readings also included minus 10 at Ohio University and Zaleski.||The 3 day cold wave finally broke that afternoon, as temperatures rose to either side of freezing with sunshine.

A warm front passed over the Ohio Valley. Due to a very cold ground, the rain showers in the wake of this warm front created a slushy layer of ice on road surfaces. While temperature sensors climbed above freezing in most locations, the rain still froze on area roadways. Numerous accidents and road closures were the result of this wintery weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain overspread a cold ground and created a slushy accumulation of ice on area roadways. Numerous accidents occurred and road closures were widespread. Over 30 accidents were reported at one point in time. Interstate 75 was shut down due to a crash involving 9 vehicles and a jackknifed semi-trailer. An airplane carrying 88 passengers slipped off of a de-icing pad at the Dayton International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system tracking northeast through Kentucky and central Ohio brought heavy snow and blizzard-like conditions to the region on January 5th. Total snow accumulations ranged between 6 and 12 inches, while 25 to 35 mph wind gusts late in the day resulted in significant blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed during the morning hours of January 5th and continued into the early morning hours of January 6th. Total snow accumulations ranged between 8 and 10 inches across the county. Winds picked up and gusted to between 25 and 35 mph by late in the afternoon and evening creating blizzard-like conditions at times. This led to snow emergencies and closings of schools and businesses the next morning as many roads became impassable.

A minus 14 was reported from both Zaleski in Vinton County and Watertown in Washington County.  Readings of minus 13 to minus 17 were reported around the town of Athens. ||The night of Jan 28th into Jan 29th was another bitter cold night with readings in interior southeast Ohio very similar to the previous night.  New Lexington reached minus 22 degrees, Beverly minus 16, Waterloo minus 13, Jackson minus 10.||With the Ohio River filled with broken ice, communities along the river were colder at dawn on the 29th.  Marietta observed 11 below zero. Newport reached down to 10 below zero, while Gallipolis fell to minus 8, and South Point reached minus 2.||Some unofficial readings from the 29th included minus 21 at Corning, minus 19 along Interstate 77 in northern Washington County, minus 18 at Ohio University in Athens, plus Zaleski in Vinton County and McConnelsville in Morgan County.  A minus 17 was reported in Wellston in Jackson County.       ||The 3rd clear night was not quite as bitter, but still resulted in temperatures dipping below zero in many areas for dawn on Thursday the 30th.  New Lexington had 10 below zero.  Some unofficial readings also included minus 10 at Ohio University and Zaleski.||The 3 day cold wave finally broke that afternoon, as temperatures rose to either side of freezing with sunshine.

An arctic cold front sweep through southeast Ohio just after midnight on Monday the 6th.   Rain showers and temperatures in the 40s quickly became snow showers with temperatures falling through the 20s by the predawn hours.||During these predawn hours, an Athens County woman with mental health issues went outside in Knollwood Trailer Park.  This mobile home park was located just southwest of the city of Athens.  She apparently slipped and fell a few times on driveways.  Detectives concluded she staggered and collapsed in a yard.  She was not found until after daybreak and likely died of exposure.||Snow accumulations across southeast Ohio were less than 2 inches.  Temperatures continued to fall during the day on the 6th, with blustery winds.  Readings reached down to either side of zero by sunset.  ||Temperatures at dawn on the 7th were mostly 5 below zero to 10 below zero.  Wind chill readings bottomed out around minus 25 degrees overnight and into the morning hours.||Despite sunshine, temperatures were slow to rise during the day on the 7th.  However, the wind did subside during the mid and late afternoon.||A scattering of frozen pipes, power outages, home furnace difficulties, and vehicular engine problems occurred during the cold wave.  Repair companies were kept busy.  School systems were closed.

A deepening low pressure system tracking northeast through Kentucky and central Ohio brought heavy snow and blizzard-like conditions to the region on January 5th. Total snow accumulations ranged between 6 and 12 inches, while 25 to 35 mph wind gusts late in the day resulted in significant blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed during the morning hours of January 5th and continued into the early morning hours of January 6th. Total snow accumulations ranged between 8 and 12 inches across the county. Winds picked up and gusted to between 25 and 35 mph by late in the afternoon and evening creating blizzard-like conditions at times. This led to snow emergencies and closings of schools and businesses the next morning as many roads became impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of cold air worked into the Ohio Valley with an upper level disturbance pivoting through the region on Sunday night, November 16th. This cold surge changed any rain that was in the area to snow during the evening hours for western Ohio, by Monday morning in central and south-central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highway department east of Eaton measured 3.5 inches of snow. A public report from in town measured 3.3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of very strong low pressure moved northeast across the Central Great Lakes on November 24th.  A cold front trailing this low swept east across Ohio during the morning and early afternoon hours.  A broken line of showers with embedded thunderstorms develop along the front as it moved into western Ohio.  A few of the stronger storms became severe and some damage was reported across north-central Ohio.   Southwest winds gusted to more than 60 mph behind the cold front causing damage throughout northern Ohio.  Hundreds of trees were reported down along with some utility poles.  Several semi trucks were blown over by the strong winds.  At least three injuries were reported during this evening.  Scattered power outages were reported in many areas.  Some of the higher wind gusts include 64 mph at Cleveland Hopkins International Airport in Cuyahoga County; 62 mph at Burke Lakefront Airport also in Cuyahoga County; 64 mph at the Lorain County Airport; 63 mph at the Wayne County Airport; 59 mph at both Toledo Express and Toledo Executive Airports in Lucas County and 62 mph at the Akron-Canton Airport in southern Summit County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds of at least 60 mph downed trees and large limbs throughout Cuyahoga County.  A few homes lost sections of roofing and siding and at least one home in Cleveland had a window blown out.  At the peak of the event, at least 19,000 utility customers were without power in the county.

A warm front passed over the Ohio Valley. Due to a very cold ground, the rain showers in the wake of this warm front created a slushy layer of ice on road surfaces. While temperature sensors climbed above freezing in most locations, the rain still froze on area roadways. Numerous accidents and road closures were the result of this wintery weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain overspread a cold ground and created a slushy accumulation of ice on area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold strong front crossed the region during the evening of February 20 into the early morning of February 21. Strong winds just off the surface increased the potential for damaging winds and isolated tornadoes along a line of showers just ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on the edge of a field along North Old State Road. A large tree and barn sustained significant damage near the beginning of the path of the tornado. The tornado then moved northeast, crossing North Old State Road. A narrow path of additional tree damage was observed in the front yard of a residence. This home sustained minor damage including a garage door blown in, loss of shingles and a couple of broken windows. A nearby utility pole and transformer were snapped with electric lines also downed. Further along the path of the tornado, a large spruce tree was uprooted, an unanchored storage shed was moved off its foundation, and several other trees were damaged. Based on the damage surveyed, the maximum estimated wind speed of this tornado was 85 miles per hour.

A cold strong front crossed the region during the evening of February 20 into the early morning of February 21. Strong winds just off the surface increased the potential for damaging winds and isolated tornadoes along a line of showers just ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down one tenth of a mile southeast of the intersection of Sweet Potato Ridge Road and Brookville Phillipsburg Road. The garage door of a residence there was blown in, leading to the removal of the roof and two walls of the garage. The roof was uplifted, flipped, and dropped in the yard immediately to the east. Several trees were also snapped in the yard immediately east of the garage. One of these trees was approximately 18 inches in diameter. The next damage location was approximately two tenths of a mile east of the intersection of Sweet Potato Ridge Road and Brookville Phillipsburg Road. A roof was partially removed from a residence on the south side of Sweet Potato Ridge Road, and blown slightly to the left of the direction of storm motion into a residence on the north side of the road. A combination of the debris and winds blew in two windows on a residence on the north side of Sweet Potato Ridge Road. Yard furniture was also lifted and carried over a tenth of a mile to the north-northeast. The tornado then removed a large portion of a barn roof on the west side of Pansing Road and carried it to the northwest, perpendicular to the direction of storm motion. Other roof debris was lifted and carried across to the east side of Pansing Road. The final damage that was observed before the tornado lifted was on Phillipsburg Union Road approximately one half mile east of the intersection with Pansing Road. This was another instance where debris from a barn was carried a short distance to the northwest of the storm motion. This debris included a portion of the roof and wood siding. Several pine trees about 8 inches in diameter were also snapped, uplifted, and dropped pointing into the direction of the storm motion. All of the damage along the path exhibited convergent motion, along with the noted distances of debris being deposited to the northwest of the original source structures. The maximum estimated wind speed based on the damage surveyed was 85 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong warm air advection in the Ohio Valley helped snow to develop in Perry County around 0300E on the 25th.  The snow then spread across the rest of southeast Ohio during the predawn hours.  Lulls in the snowfall developed during the midday.  A strong cold front and its associated mid level disturbance helped trigger more showery snow during the late afternoon into the early evening.  Snow totals of 4 to 7 inches fell across Perry and Morgan.  Meanwhile, snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were more common further south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As colder air was filtering south, a strong mid level disturbance moved up the Ohio River.  Only a weak low pressure system was reflected at the surface, which passed to the south. Snow developed in southwestern Ohio by 0000E on the 21st.  The snow moved east, into southeast Ohio the predawn.||Southeast Ohio was on the fringe of the steady snow.  Lawrence County saw accumulations of 3 to 5 inches during the daylight hours.   Further to the north in southeast Ohio, snow amounts were less, mostly 1 to 2 inches.    ||In the wake of the storm, temperatures dropped below zero for dawn on the 22nd.  Waterloo in Lawrence County was the coldest in southeast Ohio, with 7 degrees below zero.  Gallipolis saw their temperature reach 3 below zero.
